evidence: Solid double yellow center lines are a standard road marking in Brazil to indicate no-passing zones on two-way roads.
The reddish soil and extremely flat terrain are highly characteristic of the Brazilian Cerrado region, particularly in states like Mato Grosso and Goiás.
The presence of large deciduous trees with thin canopies and hazy skies suggests the dry/burning season common in the Brazilian interior.
Driving on the right side of the road is consistent with Brazilian traffic laws.
Lattice power pylons are common for high-voltage transmission across the vast agricultural plains of Brazil.
contradictions: Thailand is a candidate for red soil and double yellow lines, but it is a left-hand drive (LHD) country, which contradicts the observation suggesting driving on the right.
While Cambodia has red soil and is right-hand drive, its road infrastructure typically features single white or yellow lines rather than the double yellow markings seen in the Americas.
